content.text: Key insightPrograms are collections of instructions that manipulate data to produce a desired result.Computer programs (both in source code format and compiled) are technically data too, since they can be moved, processed, and stored. However, in the context of a computer program, we typically use the term “code” to mean the program itself, and “data” to mean the information that the program works with to produce a result.

Programs are collections of instructions that manipulate data to produce a desired result.Computer programs (both in source code format and compiled) are technically data too, since they can be moved, processed, and stored. However, in the context of a computer program, we typically use the term “code” to mean the program itself, and “data” to mean the information that the program works with to produce a result.

Computer programs (both in source code format and compiled) are technically data too, since they can be moved, processed, and stored. However, in the context of a computer program, we typically use the term “code” to mean the program itself, and “data” to mean the information that the program works with to produce a result.

list: Numbers (e.g.5or-6.7).Characters, which are placed between single-quotes (e.g.'H'or'$'). Only a single symbol may be used.Text, which must be placed between double-quotes (e.g."Hello"or"H"). Text can contain 0 or more characters.

Characters, which are placed between single-quotes (e.g.'H'or'$'). Only a single symbol may be used.Text, which must be placed between double-quotes (e.g."Hello"or"H"). Text can contain 0 or more characters.
Text, which must be placed between double-quotes (e.g."Hello"or"H"). Text can contain 0 or more characters.

content.text: Key insightValues placed in single-quotes are interpreted by the compiler as character values.Values placed in double-quotes are interpreted by the compiler as text values.Numeric values are not quoted.
Values placed in single-quotes are interpreted by the compiler as character values.Values placed in double-quotes are interpreted by the compiler as text values.Numeric values are not quoted.
Key insightAn object is used to store a value in memory. A variable is an object that has a name (identifier).Naming our objects let us refer to those objects again later in the program.
An object is used to store a value in memory. A variable is an object that has a name (identifier).Naming our objects let us refer to those objects again later in the program.
Naming our objects let us refer to those objects again later in the program.

NomenclatureIn general programming, the termobjecttypically refers to an unnamed object in memory, a variable, or a function. In C++, the termobjecthas a narrower definition that excludes functions. When we use the term object in this tutorial series, this narrower C++ definition is the one we mean.

In general programming, the termobjecttypically refers to an unnamed object in memory, a variable, or a function. In C++, the termobjecthas a narrower definition that excludes functions. When we use the term object in this tutorial series, this narrower C++ definition is the one we mean.
Key insightA definition statement can be used to tell the compiler that we want to use a variable in our program.
A definition statement can be used to tell the compiler that we want to use a variable in our program.

content.code: int x; // define a variable named x (of type int)

content.text: Key insightAn object is “created” once actual storage has been reserved for the object’s use.
An object is “created” once actual storage has been reserved for the object’s use.
Key insightThe data type of an object must be known at compile-time (so the compiler knows how much memory that object requires).
The data type of an object must be known at compile-time (so the compiler knows how much memory that object requires).

content.code: double width; // define a variable named width, of type double

int a, int b; // wrong (compiler error)

int a, b; // correct

content.text: We’ll discuss the third issue next lesson, when we cover initialization of variables.Best practiceAlthough the language allows you to do so, avoid defining multiple variables of the same type in a single statement. Instead, define each variable in a separate statement on its own line (and then use a single-line comment to document what it is used for).SummaryIn C++, we use objects to access memory. A named object is called a variable. Each variable has an identifier, a type, and a value (and some other attributes that aren’t relevant here). A variable’s type is used to determine how the value in memory should be interpreted.Variables are actually created at runtime, when memory is allocated for their use.In the next lesson, we’ll look at how to give values to our variables and how to actually use them.Quiz timeQuestion #1What is data?Show SolutionData is any information that can be moved, processed, or stored by a computer.Question #2What is a value?Show SolutionA value is a letter (e.g.a), number (e.g.5), text (e.g.Hello), or instance of some other useful concept that can be represented as data.Question #3What is an object?Show SolutionAn object is a region of storage (usually memory) that can store a value.Question #4What is a variable?Show SolutionA variable is an object that has a name.Question #5What is an identifier?Show SolutionAn identifier is the name that a variable is accessed by.Question #6What is a data type used for?Show SolutionA data type determines what kind of value (e.g. a number, a letter, text, etc…) the object will store.Question #7What is an integer?Show SolutionAn integer is a number that can be written without a fractional component.Next lesson1.4Variable assignment and initializationBack to table of contentsPrevious lesson1.2Comments

Best practiceAlthough the language allows you to do so, avoid defining multiple variables of the same type in a single statement. Instead, define each variable in a separate statement on its own line (and then use a single-line comment to document what it is used for).

Although the language allows you to do so, avoid defining multiple variables of the same type in a single statement. Instead, define each variable in a separate statement on its own line (and then use a single-line comment to document what it is used for).

SummaryIn C++, we use objects to access memory. A named object is called a variable. Each variable has an identifier, a type, and a value (and some other attributes that aren’t relevant here). A variable’s type is used to determine how the value in memory should be interpreted.Variables are actually created at runtime, when memory is allocated for their use.In the next lesson, we’ll look at how to give values to our variables and how to actually use them.Quiz timeQuestion #1What is data?Show SolutionData is any information that can be moved, processed, or stored by a computer.Question #2What is a value?Show SolutionA value is a letter (e.g.a), number (e.g.5), text (e.g.Hello), or instance of some other useful concept that can be represented as data.Question #3What is an object?Show SolutionAn object is a region of storage (usually memory) that can store a value.Question #4What is a variable?Show SolutionA variable is an object that has a name.Question #5What is an identifier?Show SolutionAn identifier is the name that a variable is accessed by.Question #6What is a data type used for?Show SolutionA data type determines what kind of value (e.g. a number, a letter, text, etc…) the object will store.Question #7What is an integer?Show SolutionAn integer is a number that can be written without a fractional component.Next lesson1.4Variable assignment and initializationBack to table of contentsPrevious lesson1.2Comments

In C++, we use objects to access memory. A named object is called a variable. Each variable has an identifier, a type, and a value (and some other attributes that aren’t relevant here). A variable’s type is used to determine how the value in memory should be interpreted.Variables are actually created at runtime, when memory is allocated for their use.In the next lesson, we’ll look at how to give values to our variables and how to actually use them.Quiz timeQuestion #1What is data?Show SolutionData is any information that can be moved, processed, or stored by a computer.Question #2What is a value?Show SolutionA value is a letter (e.g.a), number (e.g.5), text (e.g.Hello), or instance of some other useful concept that can be represented as data.Question #3What is an object?Show SolutionAn object is a region of storage (usually memory) that can store a value.Question #4What is a variable?Show SolutionA variable is an object that has a name.Question #5What is an identifier?Show SolutionAn identifier is the name that a variable is accessed by.Question #6What is a data type used for?Show SolutionA data type determines what kind of value (e.g. a number, a letter, text, etc…) the object will store.Question #7What is an integer?Show SolutionAn integer is a number that can be written without a fractional component.Next lesson1.4Variable assignment and initializationBack to table of contentsPrevious lesson1.2Comments
